scripts.title: Post-Operative Pain Assessment
scripts.scenario: Post-operative assessment following right knee replacement surgery two days ago.

noteTemplate.heading: POST-OPERATIVE NURSING ASSESSMENT
noteTemplate.subheading: Patient: David Chen | DOB: [not given] | Ward: Orthopaedic

questions.noteContext: Overnight status: Patient reports poor sleep due to ___
Pain at rest (current): ___ / 10
Pain on movement: ___ / 10
Type of pain described: sharp, ___ pain around the knee
Current analgesia: Paracetamol 1g QDS + Oxycodone 5mg PRN. Patient took oxycodone at approximately ___
Patient concern re analgesics: worried about ___ (family history – brother had problems)
Proposed pain management changes: Add anti-inflammatory + consider ___
Nerve block: described as local ___ injection near knee nerves
Swelling management: Ice pack applied – protocol: 20 min on, ___ min off
Neurovascular obs: Toes – patient reports ___ sensation
Circulation check: Pedal pulse – ___. Toes warm.
Nutritional status: Appetite ___. Nausea from anaesthetic has settled. Plan: refer to ___ if not improved by tomorrow.

scripts.title: Newly Diagnosed Type 2 Diabetes
scripts.scenario: First appointment following recent diagnosis of Type 2 diabetes from routine blood tests.

sections.title: Referral
sections.content: GP referral – elevated blood glucose on routine blood tests

questions.noteContext: Patient's emotional state: Feeling ___ about diagnosis
Family history concern: Mother had diabetes – complication: lost her ___
HbA1c result: ___ mmol/mol (normal <42; diagnostic threshold: ≥48)
Current lifestyle – Occupation: ___ work (sedentary)
Dietary advice given using the ___ method (plate divided into sections)
Plate proportions: ½ non-starchy vegetables, ¼ ___, ¼ starchy carbohydrate
Sugar intake: Patient takes ___ sugars in chai tea
Medication prescribed: ___ 500mg once daily with evening meal
Main side effect of metformin: ___ (mitigated by taking with food)
Exercise target: ___ minutes of moderate activity per week
Monitoring plan: HbA1c checks every ___ months initially
Additional appointments arranged: eye screening, ___ check, blood tests (cholesterol & kidney), diabetes education group

scripts.title: Paediatric Asthma Review

speakers.patient: Karen Morrison (mother of Lily, 7 years old)

scripts.scenario: Review following admission for acute asthma exacerbation the previous night.

noteTemplate.heading: PAEDIATRIC RESPIRATORY NURSE ASSESSMENT
noteTemplate.subheading: Patient: Lily Morrison, 7 years | Parent: Karen Morrison

sections.title: Admission
sections.content: Acute asthma exacerbation – admitted via ambulance previous night

questions.noteContext: Events preceding attack: Lily had a ___ for a couple of days before the episode
Symptom onset: Coughing began during ___
Reliever inhaler given: ___ puffs initially, then another ___ puffs 20 minutes later
Reason for calling ambulance: Child struggling to breathe + lips appeared ___
Preventer inhaler (brown): Prescribed ___ months ago but NOT used regularly
Reason preventer not used: Child seemed ___ and resists taking it when not symptomatic
Nurse's analogy for preventer: Compared to putting on ___ before going out in the sun
Spacer use: Patient has one at home but doesn't use it – says it's ___
Written asthma action plan: Previously provided by ___ but now lost
Action plan copies needed for: home and ___
Discharge plan: Expected to go home this ___
Follow-up: GP appointment within the next ___

scripts.title: Elderly Fall Risk Assessment
scripts.scenario: Home visit following GP referral after a fall two weeks ago. Daughter has concerns about safety.

sections.title: Referral
sections.content: GP referral post-fall – daughter concerned about patient's safety living alone

questions.noteContext: Cause of fall: Foot caught on edge of ___ in kitchen
Injury sustained: Hit ___ on corner of table
Time on floor before getting up: Approximately ___ minutes
Daughter's suggestion: Wants patient to wear a personal ___ necklace
Medications (5): antihypertensive, diuretic, statin, arthritis medication, and ___
Reason for insomnia: Since husband ___ (18 months ago)
Symptom reported: ___ when standing up (holds on to something until it passes)
BP readings – Sitting: 148/82 | Standing: ___/70
Diagnosis suggested: Postural ___
Last eye test: Approximately ___ years ago
Home hazards identified: Kitchen rug + loose ___ in living room
Referrals planned: Home safety assessment, ___ (for balance and strength exercises), personal alarm

scripts.title: Prenatal Care – Gestational Diabetes Screening

speakers.patient: Maria Santos, 34 years old, 28 weeks pregnant

scripts.scenario: Antenatal appointment to discuss glucose tolerance test results showing gestational diabetes. Second pregnancy.

noteTemplate.heading: ANTENATAL CONSULTATION
noteTemplate.subheading: Patient: Maria Santos, 34 | Gestation: 28 weeks | G2P1

questions.noteContext: Patient's current symptoms: Feeling very ___
Gestational diabetes prevalence: Affects approximately 1 in ___ pregnancies
Risk factors identified: Age >30, family history (father has Type 2 diabetes), BMI at booking: ___
Blood glucose monitoring frequency: ___ times per day
Target blood glucose – Fasting: below ___
Target blood glucose – 1 hour post-meal: below ___
Dietary advice: Switch from white to ___ rice, reduce portion to fist-sized
Referral to ___ for culturally appropriate dietary advice
If diet fails after ___ weeks: Consider medication (metformin or insulin)
Appointment frequency increased to every ___ weeks
Additional growth scans planned at ___ and 36 weeks
Post-delivery: Blood sugar tested ___ to ___ weeks after birth; annual screening recommended due to increased risk of Type 2 diabetes

scripts.title: Cardiac Rehabilitation Assessment
scripts.scenario: Initial cardiac rehab assessment, 3 weeks post-MI with PCI and stent insertion.

sections.title: Cardiac event
sections.content: MI + PCI with stent insertion – 3 weeks ago

questions.noteContext: Patient's main concern: Intense ___ about recurrence
Chest twinges: Type of pain – ___, related to movement (likely musculoskeletal)
Original MI pain described as: Heavy ___
Current activity level: Walks to end of ___ and back only
Previous hobbies stopped: ___ (twice weekly) and walking the dog
Rehab programme: Runs ___ times per week for ___ weeks
Fitness benchmark for resuming sexual activity: Able to climb ___ flights of stairs without symptoms
Medications (5): aspirin, second antiplatelet, statin, ___, and ACE inhibitor
Beta-blocker side effect reported: ___
Critical medication warning: Never stop ___ without medical advice (protects stent)
Occupation: ___ (desk-based but stressful)
Return to work plan: Phased return suggested, starting ___

scripts.title: Mental Health Assessment – Post-Natal Depression

speakers.patient: Natalie Barnes, 29 years old, first-time mother

scripts.scenario: Routine 6-week postnatal check. Health visitor identifies signs of postnatal depression.

noteTemplate.subheading: Patient: Natalie Barnes, 29 | Baby: Oliver, 6 weeks | G1P1

questions.noteContext: Baby Oliver: Mother reports he is growing well and is a ___ baby
Emotional state: Describes feeling like she's ___
Duration of symptoms: Past ___ to ___ weeks (initially attributed to baby blues)
Sleep: Cannot sleep even when ___
Social withdrawal: Stopped answering friends' ___
Family support: Mother offered to ___. Patient declined – doesn't want her to see her like this.
Appetite: Poor – ___ quite a bit of weight
Safety assessment: No thoughts of self-harm, but believes Oliver would be ___ without her
Diagnosis: Health visitor identifies likely ___ depression
Treatment options discussed: talking therapy, ___, or combination
Support group: Local ___ support group available (patient not ready yet)
Immediate plan: GP appointment this ___. Health visitor to increase visit frequency.

Likely cause: New ___ that were too tight
Wound measurements: Approximately ___ cm × 2 cm
Wound location: Plantar surface beneath first ___ head
Wound bed: Mix of granulation tissue and ___ areas. No signs of infection.
Neuropathy: Patient reports numbness in feet for ___ years (peripheral neuropathy)
Vascular assessment: ABPI right side = ___
HbA1c (4 months ago): ___ mmol/mol – poorly controlled
Diabetes medications: Currently on ___ different tablets
Wound management: Debridement of sloughy tissue + ___ dressing applied
Dressing change frequency: Every ___ to ___ days
Critical intervention: Offloading via therapeutic ___

scripts.title: Infection Control in Hospital Settings
scripts.scenario: Talk to nursing students on current best practices in hospital infection control.

questions.questionText: According to the speaker, approximately how many hospital patients worldwide are affected by healthcare-associated infections?
What does the speaker say is the typical hand hygiene compliance rate in most hospitals?
The speaker identifies several barriers to hand hygiene compliance. Which does she describe as a 'critical' barrier?

options.A: Lack of training for new staff

questions.questionText: According to the speaker, what is the single most effective intervention for reducing catheter-related urinary tract infections?

options.B: Daily review of whether the catheter is still needed

questions.questionText: The speaker mentions antimicrobial stewardship as a nursing responsibility. This does NOT include:

options.A: Questioning whether an antibiotic is indicated
options.C: Ensuring doses are given on time

questions.questionText: What is the speaker's main message to the nursing students?

options.A: Infection control is primarily the responsibility of specialist teams
options.B: Technology will eventually solve most infection control problems
options.C: Every healthcare worker shares responsibility for infection prevention
options.D: New antibiotics are the most promising solution to HCAIs

scripts.title: Managing Chronic Pain in Older Adults
scripts.scenario: Conference presentation on challenges and best practices of chronic pain management in elderly patients.

questions.questionText: What percentage of adults over 65 does the speaker say experience persistent pain?
The speaker recommends the Abbey Pain Scale for patients with dementia. What does this tool assess?

options.B: Observable behaviours such as vocalisation and facial expression
options.C: Blood pressure and heart rate changes

questions.questionText: According to the speaker, why should NSAIDs be used with caution in the elderly?

options.A: They are ineffective for most types of pain
options.B: They interact with all common elderly medications
options.C: They carry risks of GI bleeding, renal impairment, and cardiovascular events
options.D: They are only available by prescription

questions.questionText: What does the speaker say should ALWAYS accompany the prescription of opioids in elderly patients?
Which non-pharmacological approach does the speaker say has been shown to be effective even in patients over 80?
What does the speaker describe as a 'fatalistic view' that needs to change?

options.A: That opioids are too dangerous for elderly patients
options.B: That chronic pain always requires medication
options.C: That pain is an inevitable part of ageing
options.D: That elderly patients cannot describe their pain accurately

scripts.title: Childhood Obesity: A Nursing Perspective
scripts.scenario: Presentation on the role of nurses in addressing childhood obesity.

questions.questionText: By age eleven, what proportion of children does the speaker say are overweight or obese in England?
According to the speaker, what is the PRIMARY reason health professionals find it difficult to address childhood obesity?

options.B: Weight is a deeply personal and sensitive subject

questions.questionText: The speaker recommends 'Motivational Interviewing.' What approach does this use?

options.A: Providing written meal plans and exercise targets
options.B: Setting strict goals with regular weigh-ins
options.C: Open-ended questions, reflective listening, and affirmations
options.D: Group education sessions with other families

questions.questionText: According to the speaker, children from the most deprived areas are how much more likely to be obese?
The speaker says weight management programmes are most effective when they:

options.A: Focus on the child's diet exclusively

questions.questionText: What THREE roles does the speaker identify for nurses in addressing childhood obesity?

options.B: Identifying at-risk children, engaging families, and advocating for broader changes

scripts.title: Nurse-Led Smoking Cessation Programmes
scripts.scenario: Presentation on implementing effective smoking cessation interventions.

questions.questionText: By what percentage does the speaker say nursing-led interventions increase the likelihood of quitting?
The 'Very Brief Advice' model has three components. What are they?
According to the speaker, which is the most effective single pharmacological agent for smoking cessation?
How does the speaker describe the effectiveness of combination therapy (e.g., patch + mouth spray) compared to single-product NRT?
Patients who receive BOTH medication AND behavioural support are how many times more likely to quit than those who try unaided?

options.A: One and a half to two times

questions.questionText: What is the speaker's overall message about smoking cessation and nursing practice?

options.A: Only specialist nurses should provide smoking cessation advice
options.B: E-cigarettes should be the first-line recommendation
options.C: Every patient contact is an opportunity to address smoking
options.D: Pharmacotherapy is more important than behavioural support

scripts.title: Palliative Care: Communication and Comfort
scripts.scenario: Talk on the nursing role in palliative care communication and symptom management.

questions.questionText: What did the patient quoted at the beginning of the talk say she was afraid of?
According to the speaker, what has early palliative care been shown to do in some studies?

options.B: Replace the need for curative treatment

questions.questionText: What skill does the speaker say is 'one of the most valuable' for nurses in palliative care?

options.A: The ability to provide medical explanations
options.B: The ability to sit with uncertainty and distress
options.C: The ability to manage complex medications
options.D: The ability to coordinate multidisciplinary teams

questions.questionText: Why does the speaker say advance care planning conversations should happen early?

options.B: Families need time to agree among themselves
options.C: The patient needs to be well enough to participate fully
options.D: Hospital policies require it within 48 hours of admission

questions.questionText: For managing breathlessness, which NON-pharmacological intervention does the speaker mention first?
What does the speaker say about using opioids for breathlessness?

options.A: They should only be used as a last resort
options.B: They are not effective for breathlessness
options.C: Used appropriately, they do not shorten life
options.D: They should be avoided in palliative patients

scripts.title: Medication Safety and Error Prevention
scripts.setting: Induction session for newly qualified nurses
scripts.scenario: Presentation on medication errors, their causes, and strategies for prevention.

questions.questionText: How many deaths per year in the UK does the speaker say are caused by medication errors?
According to the speaker, what causes the majority of medication errors?
What does 'APINCH' stand for in the context of high-risk medications?

options.A: Antibiotics, Paracetamol, Insulin, Narcotics, Calcium, Heparin
options.B: Anti-infectives, Potassium, Insulin, Narcotics, Chemotherapy, Heparin
options.C: Analgesics, Psychotropics, Immunosuppressants, NSAIDs, Corticosteroids, Hormones
options.D: Anticoagulants, Pressors, Inotropes, Neuromuscular blockers, Cytotoxics, Hypoglycaemics

questions.questionText: Beyond the traditional 'five rights,' which additional check does the speaker suggest?
According to research cited by the speaker, each interruption during medication preparation does what to the risk of error?
What does the speaker identify as a risk introduced by electronic prescribing technology?

options.B: Alert fatigue leading to overriding warnings

scripts.title: Mental Health First Aid for Nursing Staff
scripts.scenario: Presentation on recognising and responding to mental health issues among nursing colleagues.

questions.questionText: According to the RCN survey cited by the speaker, what percentage of nursing staff reported feeling unwell due to work-related stress?
The speaker lists signs of mental health distress in colleagues. Which is NOT one she mentions?
In the mental health first aid approach, what does the speaker say you should do FIRST?
According to the speaker, what is a major barrier preventing nurses from seeking help for mental health issues?

options.C: Fear of being deemed unfit to practise
options.D: Not knowing where to find help

questions.questionText: What does the speaker mean when she says 'You cannot yoga your way out of chronic understaffing'?

options.A: Yoga is not an effective stress management technique
options.B: Individual self-care is not sufficient when problems are systemic
options.C: Nurses should not practise yoga during work hours
options.D: Management should provide yoga classes instead of addressing understaffing

questions.questionText: What is the speaker's call to action at the end of the presentation?

options.A: Sign up for a mental health first aid course
options.B: Report any colleague showing signs of distress to HR
options.C: Ask a colleague you've been worried about if they are okay
options.D: Lobby management for better working conditions
